notes: Coverage is the last 7 days in Asia/Taipei.
Google-account-backed workbook observation used Codex in-app Browser only; no Google connector/API fallback was used.
Items are deduplicated against the prior week by id and source URL; this week contains six new items.

highlights.title: Google-backed automation evidence moved to visible browser context
highlights.why: The 2026-06-26 connector freeze changes the operating proof for weekly data refresh: account, URL, file context, and browser-export evidence now matter more than Drive connector metadata.
highlights.next: Keep source-mode notes in every weekly run and stop with google_browser_blocked/google_account_wrong if the visible browser cannot prove the intended account.

highlights.title: Codex Remote general availability makes mobile approval a real operations pattern
highlights.why: Codex Remote GA adds authenticated phone-to-host control and approval, which is directly relevant to Kevin's recurring automation and remote review workflow.
highlights.next: Use it only with explicit approval gates and host/account context recorded before any external action.

highlights.title: Work-rhythm add-on now has aggregate InfoCenter signals
highlights.why: The InfoCenter helper scanned 500 events and generated 72-person aggregate rhythm signals without publishing raw event titles or comments.
highlights.next: Use rhythm only for assignment timing and check-in planning, not reimbursement or payroll evidence.

items.title: Google plugin freeze makes visible browser evidence the default source mode
items.oneLine: Google-account-backed observations must now happen through the Codex in-app Browser, with account and target context recorded, not through Google connectors.
facts: This run opened the intern workbook in the Codex in-app Browser and confirmed the visible account as egroup.kevin@gmail.com.
The browser URL was https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1X1bF9FIhqsOhVLl9nLfewoPuivfGKdvb/edit?gid=550857600#gid=550857600 and the visible file title was 4月先博薪資彙整.

The main workbook browser export matched the existing local XLSX SHA256 hash, while historical reimbursement browser exports were refreshed into the private workspace before rebuilding redacted outputs.
yourTake: This is the most important workflow change this week because it affects how every future Google-backed automation proves freshness.
The practical rule is source-mode accounting: visible browser evidence is current evidence; connector/API shortcuts are blocked unless Kevin explicitly lifts the freeze.
actions: Add browser URL, visible account, file/context, query/date range, and blocker label to future weekly run closeouts.
Treat missing visible browser access as a stopped run, not as permission to use a connector fallback.

sources.label: Visible Codex in-app Browser - Google Sheets workbook

credibility.reason: Observed directly in the approved in-app Browser with the expected Google account visible.

followups: Next run should verify whether browser-visible modified-time evidence can be captured without using Google connector metadata.

items.title: MOHW social-work staffing and training notices remain the capacity watch point
items.oneLine: MOHW social-work related public signals this week are operational: training windows, staffing, and unit capacity rather than a major policy shift.
facts: The specialist social-work training organization application window still closes on 2026-06-29.
MOHW public notice pages continue to show social-welfare and social-work unit staffing/activity signals rather than a major new policy change in this week window.
yourTake: For Kevin's workforce lens, this is a no-drama but actionable signal: staffing and training pipeline readiness matter more than headline policy this week.
The useful artifact is a partner checklist for training capacity and supervision readiness, not another generic news summary.
actions: Before 2026-06-29, keep the specialist-training organization requirements in the partner-review checklist.
Recheck after 2026-07-02 for any follow-up notice or application result signal.

sources.label: MOHW specialist social-work training organization recognition notice

credibility.reason: Primary official sources, but the week produced continuity signals rather than a major new social-work policy release.

followups: Check whether the June 29 window creates any July follow-up notice.

items.title: FSC eNotice expansion points to consumer document-readiness education
items.oneLine: FSC news this week included eNotice-related consumer-finance infrastructure, which is a practical trigger for plain-language account/document education.
facts: FSC's news center carried a June 2026 item on electronic notice and financial-market infrastructure.
The consumer education angle is not product promotion: it is helping people know which official notices matter, where they arrive, and how to avoid missing account or rights-related messages.
yourTake: This is useful for financial education because e-notice adoption usually creates confusion around official channels, scams, and missed deadlines.
A good FamilyFin-style output would be a short checklist, not a technical market-infrastructure explainer.
actions: Watch for FSC/TDCC FAQ material and only then draft a consumer-facing checklist.
Frame the checklist around safe official channels, what to archive, and when to ask a financial institution for help.

credibility.reason: Primary FSC news list; final consumer checklist should wait for a more specific FAQ or release page.

followups: Recheck FSC/TDCC for a dedicated eNotice consumer FAQ by 2026-07-10.

items.title: Codex Remote reached general availability
items.oneLine: OpenAI says Codex Remote is GA, allowing work to start or continue from the ChatGPT mobile app on a connected Mac or Windows host.
facts: The 2026-06-25 Codex changelog says Codex Remote reached general availability.
Remote Control now uses authenticated one-to-one QR pairing between each iOS or Android device and each host.
Connections used since 2026-06-08 remain paired, while older inactive connections need to pair again.
yourTake: This makes phone-based review and approval a mainstream Codex workflow pattern, not just an experiment.
For Kevin's automations, the key is not convenience; it is preserving approval mode, host identity, and action scope when review happens away from the desktop.
actions: Document which automation actions may be approved remotely and which still require desktop/browser-visible context.
If Codex Remote is used for Google-account-backed work, still require visible target context before account-backed observation or writes.
followups: Add Codex Remote to the next automation approval-boundary review if Kevin starts using it operationally.

items.title: InfoCenter work-rhythm add-on produced aggregate-only planning signals
items.oneLine: The read-only InfoCenter helper generated a public-safe aggregate rhythm file for assignment timing and support planning.
facts: The helper scanned 500 InfoCenter events and generated signals for 72 people.
The output file is data/work-rhythm.internal.json and excludes raw event titles, comment bodies, salary amounts, bank data, ID numbers, and full addresses.
The current run reported 480 total signals and 0 high-confidence signals.
yourTake: This is useful as a staffing-support lens, not as a payroll or reimbursement source of truth.
Low or medium confidence should prompt human check-in before assignment decisions, not automatic routing.
actions: Review the work-rhythm page for whether the aggregate labels are useful enough for Kevin's weekly assignment planning.
Keep reimbursement sheets as authoritative for work records and pay status.

sources.label: InfoCenter event list - read-only browser/profile helper

credibility.reason: Live helper read succeeded, but the output is aggregate rhythm evidence only and intentionally not raw audit evidence.

followups: Check whether the work-rhythm route is understandable without exposing raw private event data.

items.title: Retention-loop watch shifts from reward mechanics to manual proof
items.oneLine: After last week's Discord reward-loop signal, the useful next step is a manual weekly challenge prototype rather than more platform scanning.
facts: Last week's Nitro Rewards/Orbs item remains the clearest recent growth-pattern source in the current watchlist.
No new higher-confidence public product pattern displaced it during this run.
yourTake: The insight should now move from radar to experiment: one manual loop can test whether people repeat, reply, or complete before any tooling exists.
For FamilyFin or member education, a low-friction non-monetary status signal is safer than points, cash, or complex badges.
actions: Write one paper prototype for a weekly challenge with completion proof and non-monetary status.
Define one success measure: repeat participation, reply rate, saved checklist, or consultation click.

credibility.reason: Primary product-pattern source from the prior week; this item is a follow-through action signal rather than a new external announcement.

followups: Decide whether to run one manual challenge before building any gamification UI.
